exercises.title: Classify the AI Tool
exercises.instructions: I'm going to describe three tools. For each one, tell me whether it's a chatbot, an AI agent, or traditional automation. Explain your reasoning.

scenarios.description: A customer support widget on a website that matches your question to a list of FAQ answers and shows the closest match.
scenarios.explanation: This is a chatbot — it pattern-matches to pre-written answers. It doesn't take actions, use tools, or adapt its approach based on results.

scenarios.description: A coding assistant that reads your project files, identifies bugs, writes fixes, runs tests to verify them, and commits the changes.
scenarios.explanation: This is an AI agent — it perceives (reads files), reasons (identifies bugs), acts (writes fixes), uses tools (runs tests), and iterates based on results.

scenarios.description: A script that runs every night at midnight, downloads sales data from an API, generates a CSV report, and emails it to the team.
scenarios.explanation: This is traditional automation — it follows a fixed sequence with no decision-making or adaptation. If the API changes, it breaks rather than adapting.

exercises.title: Trace the Agent Loop
exercises.instructions: I'll describe what an agent does in a scenario. Break it down into the steps of the agent loop: Perceive, Reason, Act, Observe. There may be multiple cycles.

scenarios.description: A user asks: 'Find all TODO comments in this codebase and create a summary.' The agent searches all files for TODO comments, groups them by priority, generates a markdown summary, and presents it to the user.

expectedBreakdown.action: Reads the user's request — goal is to find and summarize TODOs
Plans approach: search for TODO patterns, then group and summarize
Uses search/grep tool to find all TODO comments across files
Gets list of 15 TODO comments across 8 files
Decides to group by priority/category for clarity
Generates a structured markdown summary organized by priority
Presents the summary to the user

exercises.title: Improve This Prompt
exercises.instructions: Here's a prompt someone wrote for an AI agent. Rewrite it to be clearer and more effective. Then I'll give you feedback on your revision.

scenarios.context: The user has a Python Flask app with a broken login endpoint that returns a 500 error.
scenarios.exampleGoodPrompt: The login endpoint in app.py is returning a 500 error when I POST to /api/login with valid credentials. Can you read app.py and the auth module, identify what's causing the server error, and fix it? Run the tests in test_auth.py after to make sure it works.
scenarios.whyBetter: Specifies: what's broken (login endpoint), the symptom (500 error), when it happens (valid credentials), where to look (app.py, auth module), what success looks like (tests pass).

scenarios.context: The user has a React app where the product listing page takes 8 seconds to load.
scenarios.exampleGoodPrompt: The product listing page at /products loads in about 8 seconds. I suspect it's re-rendering too many components or fetching too much data upfront. Can you profile the ProductList component, check for unnecessary re-renders, and look at the API call in useProducts.ts to see if we can paginate or lazy-load?
scenarios.whyBetter: Specifies: what's slow (product listing), how slow (8 seconds), suspected causes (re-renders, data fetching), specific files to check, and potential solution approaches.

exercises.title: Spot the Anti-Pattern
exercises.instructions: Each of these prompts contains a prompting anti-pattern. Identify which anti-pattern it is and explain why it's problematic.

scenarios.prompt: Refactor the auth module, also add dark mode to the UI, and while you're at it check if there are any security vulnerabilities in our dependencies.
scenarios.explanation: Three unrelated tasks crammed into one message. The agent will likely do a mediocre job on all three. Each should be a separate request so the agent can focus.

scenarios.prompt: The thing we discussed yesterday isn't working. Can you fix it?
scenarios.explanation: Assumes the agent remembers a previous conversation (it likely doesn't). Doesn't specify what 'the thing' is or what 'not working' means. The agent has no context to act on.

scenarios.prompt: Build me a REST API for user management. Actually, make it GraphQL. Wait, can it support both?
scenarios.explanation: Requirements change three times in one message. The agent doesn't know which to follow. Decide on requirements first, then give a clear, stable instruction.

exercises.title: Match the Task to the Tool
exercises.instructions: For each task, identify which agent tools would be needed. Available tools: file read, file write, web search, shell/terminal, code search (grep), API call.

scenarios.task: Find all functions in the codebase that handle user authentication.
scenarios.explanation: Code search to find auth-related functions, then file read to examine them in context.

scenarios.task: Check if the latest version of the 'requests' library has any known security issues.
scenarios.explanation: Web search to check vulnerability databases, or shell to run 'pip audit' or similar tools.

scenarios.task: Create a new configuration file based on the existing template.
scenarios.explanation: File read to examine the template, file write to create the new config.

exercises.title: Spot the Problem in Agent Output
exercises.instructions: I'll show you output from an AI agent. Your job is to identify any issues — errors, missing steps, assumptions, or things you'd want to verify before accepting the work.

scenarios.agentOutput: I've updated the database schema to add a `role` column to the users table and deployed the migration to production.
issues: Deployed to production without review — this should go through staging first
No mention of a rollback plan if the migration fails
No mention of whether existing rows get a default value for the new column
No mention of updating application code that reads the users table
scenarios.teachingPoint: Always check: Did the agent skip steps? Did it take irreversible actions? Did it handle data migration for existing records?

exercises.title: Design a Multi-Step Workflow
exercises.instructions: You need an agent to accomplish this complex task. Decide: what high-level goal do you give the agent, and what checkpoints would you set where you review its plan before it continues?

scenarios.task: Migrate a Node.js Express API from JavaScript to TypeScript, including updating all tests.

exampleApproach.highLevelGoal: Migrate the Express API to TypeScript. Start by converting the data models, then routes, then middleware. Update tests for each module after converting it. Don't change any business logic — this is a type-safety migration only.
checkpoints: After planning: Review the migration order and any dependency issues
After models: Verify type definitions match current behavior
After routes: Run tests to ensure nothing broke
After middleware: Final full test suite run

scenarios.teachingPoint: Good delegation: clear goal, clear scope boundaries ('don't change business logic'), and checkpoints where you verify before continuing.

exercises.title: Agent or No Agent?
exercises.instructions: For each task, decide: should you use an AI agent, or is there a simpler approach? Explain your reasoning.

scenarios.task: Rename a variable from 'usr' to 'user' across 3 files.
scenarios.explanation: Find-and-replace in your editor is faster and more reliable. An agent adds overhead for a mechanical task.

scenarios.task: Investigate why the test suite is 3x slower than it was last month and suggest optimizations.
scenarios.explanation: This requires investigation (reading git history, profiling tests, comparing changes), reasoning about causes, and synthesizing findings. An agent excels here.

scenarios.task: Add a copyright header to every source file in the project.
scenarios.explanation: A simple shell script or editor macro handles this perfectly. No judgment or reasoning required.

scenarios.task: Review a pull request with 15 changed files across the frontend and backend, checking for bugs, performance issues, and style consistency.
scenarios.explanation: Multi-file review requiring understanding of both frontend and backend, checking multiple dimensions. This is judgment-heavy work where agents add value.
